**Neuromodulation of Mouse Auditory Cortex Using Focused Ultrasound**  
***Jacob Hehir, Fisher Lab, Department of Physiology, NYMC***  
**Abstract**

Neurological diseases impose a large health and financial burden on patients around the world. Current treatments for conditions such as Parkinson’s disease are limited to pharmacology or invasive surgical approaches like deep brain stimulation. For conditions mediated by dysfunctional cortical activity, like tinnitus and drug-resistant epilepsy (DRE), treatments are in dire need. Neuromodulation is an alternative therapy where electromagnetic or ultrasonic pulses are used to non-invasively modify the activity of neurons. Focused ultrasound (FUS) has been shown to elicit, enhance, and suppress neural activity at cortical and subcortical targets with much higher spatial resolution than other neuromodulation techniques such as transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) and transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS). If we can determine the parameters necessary to safely induce desired changes in neural activity, FUS can serve as a powerful non-invasive tool for both the treatment and study of brain function in humans.   
Previous studies have shown that FUS can modulate cortical responses to sensory stimuli for extended periods of time following treatment. However, these measurements have been limited to recordings of evoked potentials which lack the spatial resolution of the FUS focus itself.  Here, we use a novel experimental approach combining wide-field calcium imaging with a scanning, low-intensity FUS transducer to assess neuromodulation at distinct spatial targets in the auditory cortex of mice. The tonotopic organization of the auditory cortex in both mice and humans provides an attractive and translatable testing ground for neuromodulation with FUS.  We used a transgenic mouse line expressing the Thy1-GCaMP6s calcium reporter to visualize fluorescence changes in primary auditory cortex (A1) and associated auditory regions.   
With this approach we were able to quantify changes in peak intensity, peak latency, and propagation of the cortical response to acoustic stimuli in mice treated with varying levels of FUS. Results show that FUS delivered at 1.2W/cm2 Isppa and 50% duty cycle (DC) induced enhancement of the peak fluorescence intensity. Mice treated with 0.6W/cm2 Isppa, 50%DC did not show significant suppression or enhancement. Results also show changes in peak latency at both levels of FUS treatment, though with high variability within treatment groups.   
FUS delivered at higher frequencies can focus with greater spatial resolution, but have been shown to experience attenuation through the skull. FUS may also cause indirect activation of peripheral auditory pathways through bone-resonance. We use an ultrasound frequency (750kHz) capable of producing a focus of 0.5mm delivered through acoustically-transparent windows to avoid perturbation of the FUS pulses and to prevent off-target activation through resonance. We demonstrate our transducer’s targeting capabilities *in situ* with heat reactive liquid-crystalline thermochromic sheets and provide exact parameters for the pressures produced at the FUS focus with hydrophone calibration.  
FUS can change the permeability of the blood brain barrier and may cause damage at sufficiently high intensities. To assess FUS damage on neural tissue and microvasculature, we examined brain slices taken from sonicated mice injected with Evans blue dye. Results confirm that high intensity FUS could induce localized damage at the targeted cortical region but show that the low intensities we used for calcium-imaging experiments did not produce damage or perturb the blood brain barrier. We also used optical coherence tomography-angiography (OCT-A) imaging of microvasculature to assess gross changes in vasodilation/vessel integrity. No gross changes were observed shortly after sonication or 24 hours after sonication in the region targeted with low intensity FUS. Finally, we assessed for changes in mouse behavior following FUS application to the cortex that may arise from unintended effects of the FUS pulse on subcortical targets. Mice did not behave differently if sonicated compared to non-sonicated mice on open-field testing (OFT).   
This work provides the first evidence of FUS neuromodulatory enhancement of sensory evoked activity to be measured with wide-field calcium images of live cortex. In addition, we employed a novel combination of imaging and scanning-FUS transducer technologies to demonstrate unimpeded ultrasonic transduction at precise targets within the mouse auditory cortex. We used three methods to measure potential FUS-induced damage and found no harmful effects when using low intensities. With this work, we contribute to the understanding of ultrasound induced neuromodulation by providing a unique approach for testing FUS effects *in vivo* and by reporting the effects we achieved at different ultrasound parameters in a complex cortical environment.

**Chapter 1: Literature Review**   
EXPAND THIS HEALTH RELEVANCE INTO A SHORT PARAGRAPH ON THE BURDEN TO THE US AND GLOBALLY TO MEDICATION-RESISTANT NEUROLOGICAL CONDITIONS.  
Neurological disorders are a growing global health issue, representing the second leading cause of death and the leading cause of disability in the 10 most populous countries world wide (Feigin et al. 2019). The prevalence of Parkinson’s Disease (PD) in the US alone has been projected to increase by 9 million patients by 2030 from 2010 levels (Bach et al. 2011). For some neurological conditions like epilepsy, an alarming proportion of patients continue to respond poorly to multiple pharmacological treatments. Drug-resistant epilepsy (DRE) affects 30% of patients diagnosed with epilepsy in the US, resulting in debilitating seizures and a 2-10 times higher risk of sudden death (Kalilani et al 2018, Fatturosso et al. 2021). Pharmacological interventions for neurological and neuropsychiatric diseases, when available, regularly produce off-target effects and can require larger doses over time to achieve therapeutic effect. While surgery can be used to ablate diseased neural tissue, such a process is invasive and irreversible. Neuromodulation provides a solution to the growing need for new and effective treatments for these conditions with some methods having the added benefit of being non-invasive.   
*Make the case for alternatives to drugs. Pretty sure you have this text scattered here and there, put that all here. I’ve cut/pasted some of the relevant text.*  
***Neuromodulation: An Overview***  
Many tools and methods have been used to change the behavior of neurons for scientific and medical pursuits. The goal driving this neural modulation can range from testing the function of certain neurons within a brain region to therapeutically adjusting the behavior of target neurons in the central or peripheral nervous system. When modulating neural behavior for the purpose of understanding or mapping functional subregions of the brain, invasive but powerful techniques offer precise control at the level of ion channels, as is the case in optogenetics (Deisseroth et al 2006). The invasiveness of neuromodulation techniques is obvious in *in vitro* preparations of excised nerves or cell cultures where cells can be activated with electrodes inserted in and around cell bodies to initiate activity (Hodgkin and Huxley, 1958, Mihran et al 1990, Kraiche et al 2008, Histed et al 2013). Such studies have provided great insight into the functional properties of individual neurons, paving the way for similar methods of neural stimulation in intact brains to understand cortical circuitry. Techniques like optogenetics, microstimulation, and local pharmacological inactivation have incredible spatial resolution, allowing for the manipulation of neural activity at the cellular level. 

For experiments on live animal models, invasive methods include stimulation with microelectrodes (Histed et al 2013), injection of pharmacological agents directly into the target brain region (Poland et al 2015), and the overexpression of light-sensitive or mechano-sensitive ion channels through genetic modification (Herlitze et al 2007, Soloperto et al 2018). These methods are useful for the study of intact brain regions in animal models, but have major barriers to use in humans for either experimental or therapeutic purposes. Genetic manipulation of the germ line is certainly not an option for healthy human subjects or as a treatment for neurological conditions. However, gene therapy through the use of viral vectors does open the possibility for the targeted expression of light sensitive opsins, albeit with considerable technical challenges. Because of these obstacles, the tools for modulating neural behavior in the human brain are generally limited to non-invasive methodologies. For the treatment of neurological disease in humans, less invasive methods incur less risk to the patient and are preferable to available invasive methods if both are effective.   
*Now here is where you should go through different neuromodulation modalities. Organization is somewhat arbitrary, here is one possibility. I would not separate the modalities based on in vivo vs. in vitro.*  
***Optogenetics***  
Optogenetics is a technique that involves the use of light-sensitive ion-channels or ion-pumps that are embedded within target neurons and stimulated with specific wave-lengths of light. The discovery of light-sensitive proteins or “opsins” capable of conducting ions has led to their use in inducing hyper-polarization or depolarization in the target cells. Opsins, derived from both bacteria and eukaryotes, have been identified and characterized by their distinct kinetic properties, polarization effects, and wavelength sensitivity (Kalanithi et al. 2012). They have been used to induce action potentials, suppress/silence neural activity, and promote an excitatory state in target cells (Deisseroth et al 2006, Herlitze et al 2007). The expression of opsins in target neurons is achieved with the use of viral vectors, such as lentivirus and adeno-associated virus (AAV), or through the creation of transgenic mouse lines (Henckaerts et al. 2008, Dittgen et al 2004). The stimulation of opsins is achieved with optic fibers that carry light from a laser or LED light source. Light-carrying fibers are implanted near the target tissue to deliver light of appropriate wavelength within the vicinity of the target cells. While this neuromodulation method provides genetic tools to control the expression of the effector to specific cell types, there are two key challenges that limit its translational application in humans. Firstly, the expression of optically sensitive proteins in target neurons requires stereotactically guided, surgical implantation of viral vectors (Kalanithi et al. 2012). With this invasive procedure comes the risk of surgical complications, unsuccessful expression, and improper mutagenesis at the insertion site (Williams and Denison 2013, Duebel et al. 2015). Secondly, the major drawbacks of using light as an activator are the proximity and/or power necessary for the light to reach the opsin with sufficient energy to produce a response. Opsins are typically sensitive to wavelengths ranging from 400nm-600nm and light within these ranges experiences large amounts of absorption and scattering as it travels through neural tissue. Achieving proper illumination at the target cortical regions in brains with large volumes will require fiber implantation and high amounts of energy to produce required levels of light, thereby complicating the design of stimulation devices (Williams and Denison 2013). 

***Deep Brain Stimulation***  
Deep brain stimulation (DBS) involves the surgical implantation of electrodes at targets deep within the brain to deliver patterns of electrical stimulation to specific subcortical targets. Though invasive, DBS has become the standard of care treatment option for certain patients with Parkinson’s disease (PD), essential tremor, and dystonia (Lozano et al 2020). In addition, DBS is being investigated as a treatment for a range of psychiatric conditions including major depression and obsessive compulsive disorder. The most effective application of DBS to date has been its use as a treatment for PD. However, despite providing considerable benefit for patients and serving as an alternative to the prior standard of care (ablative surgery), DBS has poorly understood mechanisms and can interact with fiber tracts adjacent to thalamic targets to exacerbate motor dysfunction in some patients (Grill et al. 2004, Johnson et al. 2013, Ashkan et al. 2017). DBS can also exacerbate mood disturbances in PD patients with concomitant psychiatric conditions (Eisenstein et al 2014). Finally, as it requires invasive surgery, DBS does pose the risk of infection, bleeding, or other surgical complications (Nuttin et al 2014). 

***Transcranial Direct Current Stimulation***  
The gap in non-invasive methods to modulate neural activity was initially filled by two main technologies. Both take advantage of the electromagnetic properties of neurons by subjecting large swaths of neural tissue to either electric or magnetic fields which are applied across the cranium. Transcranial stimulation of cortical circuits can be achieved with directly applied electrical currents termed transcranial electric stimulation (tES). This is performed using direct currents (tDCS), alternating currents (tACS), or random noise currents (tRNS) applied through pads that deliver charge across the regions of cortex lying in between the anode and cathode (Terney et al. 2008, Campana et al 2016, Yang et al 2022). The traveling current is used to activate or suppress neurons whose axons are parallel to the axis of the current. The key drawback to tES is poor spatial resolution of the affected area, which has a reported minimum size of 10cm2 (Kubanek et al 2018, Faria et al 2011). Furthermore the shallow depth reached by the electric currents running from one pad to another does not allow targeting of subcortical structures when applied to large-brain volumes, such as the human brain. This poses issues for treating conditions in which the aberrant activity in need of modulation occurs with smaller foci or at subcortical locations. 

***Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation***  
Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) uses magnetic coils with varying configurations to apply magnetic fields across the skull and onto the brain to induce electrical currents in target regions (Barker et al 1985, Chen et al 2015, Chail et al 2018). Like tES, TMS can induce and suppress activity via electric currents, taking advantage of the electrochemical coupling first observed by Hodgkin and Huxley to promote or suppress action potentials during treatment (Hodgkin and Huxley, 1952). These methods can be used to induce lasting changes which are theorized to result from long term potentiation (LTP) or depression (LTD) upon repeated stimulations (Hallet et al. 2007, Terney et al. 2008, Shulz et al. 2013). TMS has been shown to consistently modulate neural activity in human experiments and has even been FDA approved as an alternative therapy for a variety of psychiatric conditions including major depressive disorder (MDD), migraines with aura, obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD), smoking cessation, and anxiety comorbid with MDD (Cohen et al 2022). TMS, however, shares the low-spatial resolution of tES, with affected regions spanning widths on the order of 1-10cm2 (Faria et al. 2011, Kubanek et al 2018). Ideally, non-invasive neuromodulation could be used to activate or suppress activity in highly localized regions of the brain both in the cerebral cortex and in deeper brain regions. This would benefit studies that seek to map the function of cortical and subcortical structures by stimulating or suppressing targets such as distinct subdivisions of the thalamus involved in sensory processing. The same is true for therapeutic applications where TMS is only able to be used in conditions that affect large populations of neurons spread out across the brain. Application to smaller targets requires considerable effort to only modulate intended structures without influencing activity in off-target regions (Romanella et al 2020). 

***Focused Ultrasound***  
	Focused ultrasound (FUS) offers the spatial resolution lacking in other non-invasive neuromodulation methodologies, being capable of focusing onto targets on the millimeter scale (Zhang et al 2021, Pasquinelli et al 2019). This method of neuromodulation can also be used to target brain regions at depths beyond the cortex, allowing for stimulation or suppression of deeper structures such as the hypothalamus (Tufail et al 2010), thalamus (Yang et al 2012, Dallapiaza et al 2018), and inferior colliculus (Fry et al 1958, Daniels et al 2018). Ultrasound itself, like audible sound, is a propagating pressure wave. We hear sound when pressure waves vibrate the tympanic membrane of the middle ear, which transduces those forces into mechanical stimulation of sensory hair cells within the cochlea, ultimately eliciting action potentials in auditory afferent nerve terminals. The biomechanical properties of the cochlea limit the range of sound frequencies that can effectively stimulate hair cells (Peterson et al. 2022). The frequency range over which sounds can elicit mechanical forces that exceed the level of thermal fluctuations in the cochlear fluids define the “audible” range. In humans, this range is roughly 20Hz-20kHz and in mice it is roughly 1kHz-100kHz (Heffner et al 2007, Reynolds et al 2010); ultrasonic frequencies are defined as those that exceed the audible range of humans, generally above 20 kHz (Zhang et al. 2021). 

***Early observations of FUS neuromodulation***  
The effects of ultrasound on nervous tissue has been reported as early as 1929, in which heart muscle responded to ultrasonic stimulation with faster and more forceful contractions (Harvey et al 1928). The major finding that led to the burgeoning field of FUS neuromodulation, though, came in 1958 when Fry and colleagues released their seminal work on the effects of FUS on the sensory response to visual stimuli *in vivo* (Fry et al 1958). In this study, FUS was applied through a craniotomy to the LGN of cats, while visually evoked potentials (VEPs) were recorded from the visual cortex via electrodes while the mouse was exposed to flashing lights. The VEPs were measured before, during, and after the application of FUS to the LGN. The striking result was that shortly after FUS treatment, the amplitude of the evoked potential was substantially reduced and eventually returned to baseline after a period of about 30 minutes. This was the first demonstration of reversible suppression via FUS neuromodulation. 

***FUS alters electrically evoked activity in peripheral nerves, hippocampal slices, and cell cultures***  
The effect of ultrasound on neural activity has been demonstrated in isolated preparations of peripheral nerves. FUS application to excised sciatic nerves enhanced both the magnitude of compound action potentials (CAPs) and the conduction velocities measured during stimulated activity (Mihran et al 1990, Tsui et al 2005). Results from early and more recent studies highlight two important characteristics of FUS’s neuromodulatory effect on peripheral nerves. Firstly, a number of studies reported both enhancement and suppression of the firing rate of action potentials (APs) after FUS application to peripheral nerves *in vitro*. The direction of modulation was dependent on the FUS intensity used, with lower intensities providing enhancement and higher intensities causing suppression (Fry et al 1950, Lele et al 1963, Colucci et al 2009, Foley et al 2006, Tsui et al 2005). The suppressive effect was largely considered to result from a thermal mechanism, as suppression was achieved with what are now considered high FUS intensities delivered over long treatment durations. This combination imparts more energy in the form of heat at the sonication focus. Secondly, the suppressive effect was shown to be reversible until sufficiently high intensities of FUS resulted in permanent suppression of the target neuron’s spontaneous activity (Fry et al, Lele et al 1963). Alternative studies provided support for non-thermal mechanisms by using a combination of FUS parameters that limited the amount of thermal energy imparted to the nerve and by measuring minimal temperature changes of  \<1°C (Mihran et al 1990). These investigations have major implications for FUS’s application as a therapeutic tool to change the behavior of peripheral nerves in, for example, a patient with chronic pain. In addition, they establish a base set of examples for an individual neuron’s responses to ultrasonic waves. For an understanding of FUS’s effects on neural circuits, however, other investigations have used hippocampal slices and nerve cells grown in culture as FUS targets.   
The response of CA1 circuitry to electrical stimulation in hippocampal slices changes after FUS application (Rinaldi et al 1991, Bachtold et al 1998). The direction of modulation observed in studies using this approach was different, depending on if the ultrasonic waves were delivered continuously or in short pulses. Continuous FUS applied to the CA1 circuitry caused a reduction in the evoked potential amplitude measured from presynaptic fibers and in the amplitude of the dendritic field potential (Rinaldi et al 1991). Pulsed FUS resulted in a similar reduction of the evoked potential amplitude of presynaptic fibers, but enhanced the dendritic field potential (Bachtold et al 1998). This highlights the importance of the parameters of the FUS being applied. Differences in the pulse-pattern and intensity of the ultrasonic waves will impart different amounts of energy to the target tissue and potentially act through different mechanisms to induce modulation (Plaskin et al 2016, Dell’Italia et al 2022).   
Studies in cell culture monolayers permitted the measurement of baseline levels of network activity for comparison before and after FUS application. FUS was shown to increase the excitability of individual neurons and dramatically increase the spike-rate of previously active neurons in the culture (Khraiche et al 2008, Kim et al 2016). In addition, FUS induced action potentials in previously silent neurons within the culture to contribute to network activity. Interestingly, FUS with considerably low acoustic intensity was still shown to enhance network activity in the target cell culture, though the onset of enhancement was delayed. Importantly, the FUS induced neuromodulatory enhancement was prevented with the application of a non-specific antagonist of glutamate receptor, kynurenic acid (KA), as well as with the application of tetrodotoxin (TTX) (Kim et al 2016). This indicated that the ability for FUS to induce neuromodulation within a cell network is dependent on the activity of glutamatergic synapses and voltage gated sodium channels.   
Similar to the early work by Fry et al in 1958 which showed suppression of the neural response to external stimuli, these studies provide evidence for FUS neuromodulation by showing changes in neural activity in response to electrical stimuli (with the addition of showing changes in spontaneous activity). Other investigations showed that FUS can not only modulate neural responses to experimental stimuli but act as a stimulus itself. 

***FUS induces neural activity in individual neurons and hippocampal circuits***  
The capacity for FUS to stimulate activity in individual cells was first demonstrated when FUS application to excised cat pacinian corpuscles produced spikes in receptor potentials (Gavrilov et al 1976). This cellular response to FUS was used to reason for the effects seen in human subjects from the same study. These subjects reported tactile sensations while their hands were stimulated with FUS, which may have been mediated through FUS modulation of mechanosensitive receptors, such as pacinian corpuscles, within the skin.   
A group of experiments by Tyler and colleagues provided a range of measurements showing FUS induced activity in hippocampal slices. Firstly, patch clamp recordings of individual neurons show FUS induced action potentials (APs) in cells within the targeted region (Tyler et al 2008). Secondly, FUS stimulation resulted in Na\+ and Ca\++ transients recorded by laser scanning confocal imaging of fluorescent reporters in targeted cells. FUS stimulation also resulted in synaptic vesicle transmission as reported by a fluorescent reporter for the activity of synaptic vesicle exocytosis machinery: Thy1-synaptopHluorin transgenic mice (Li et al 2005).  
FUS induced sodium and calcium transients were removed with TTX while calcium transients were prevented via a Cd\++ blockade of voltage gated calcium channels (Tyler et al 2008). These findings indicate that FUS acts on voltage gated sodium and calcium channels, which likely possess some level of mechanosensitivity, in order to impart its effect on the neuron’s activity (Kubanek et al 2017, Yoo et al 2022, Zhu et al 2023). FUS induced synaptic vesicle transmission was prevented by genetically cleaving the Snap-25 protein of the exocytosis machinery, indicating that FUS induced SNARE mediated exocytosis. Lastly, by inhibiting network activity with CNQX (an AMPA receptor blocker) and APV (an NMDA receptor blocker), synaptic vesicle transmission was reduced by half, indicating that FUS induces synaptic transmission rather than just forcing mass exocytosis. This thorough study provides ample evidence for FUS’s ability to induce neural activity and serves as a basis for the FUS stimulation observed in later studies that use *in vivo* approaches. 

***FUS induces activity in cortical and subcortical targets***  
	FUS has been used to induce activity in both motor cortex (M1), hippocampus, and visual cortex in multiple *in vivo* models (Tufail et al 2010, Yoo et al 2011, King et al 2013, Kim et al 2014). Activity induced by FUS has been demonstrated through increased cortical spiking in recordings of local field potentials (LFPs) and multi-unit activity (MUA) in the motor cortex of lightly anesthetized mice (Tufail et al 2010). Importantly, this FUS induced stimulation was prevented with the application of TTX, providing *in vivo* support for prior findings *in vitro* (Tyler et al 2008, Kim et al 2016). FUS applied to the hippocampus of lightly anesthetized mice also resulted in short lived LFPs with “after-discharge” activity lasting less than 3 seconds. The patterns of after-discharge activity measured with LFPs included gamma oscillations and short wave ripples (SWPs) which are characteristic patterns of hippocampal activity. This indicates that FUS induced the naturally occuring patterns of activity in the target region. These results are an important example of FUS’s ability to induce neural activity in both cortical and subcortical targets, in a live mouse model.  
 FUS stimulation of the motor cortex has been extensively observed in a variety of animal models. FUS application to motor regions in anesthetized animals has been shown to induce muscle contractions of extremities such as the limbs or tail, which were recorded visually and with electromyography (EMG) in mice (Tufail et al 2010, King et al 2013, King et al 2014, Mehic et al 2014), rats (Younan et al 2013, Kim et al 2014, Kim et al 2014), and larger animal models such as sheep (Lee et al 2016). The success rate of inducing a myogenic response with FUS varied with the acoustic intensity at the FUS focus as well as with the duty cycle (DC) used. The DC refers to the ratio of the active and inactive periods of time within the ultrasonic pulse pattern. If the DC is set to 100%, for example, that would be equivalent to continuous FUS as there are no gaps between pulses. If the DC is lowered to 50%, however, the FUS would be delivered in pulses, with each pulse containing a burst of ultrasound lasting half the length of the pulse, followed by “silence” for the other half of the pulse. The pulsed pattern of ultrasound bursts followed by periods of silence allows for the use of higher acoustic intensities without imparting large amounts of thermal energy at the FUS focus over time. The effectiveness of pulsed FUS versus continuous FUS for stimulating cortical activity is debated, though pulsed FUS with higher DCs appear more effective than FUS with lower DCs (King et al 2013, Kim et al 2014). Pulsed FUS has been shown to induce both motor and visual activity in sheep, where FUS with 50% DC delivered to V1 induced visually evoked potentials on electroencephalogram (EEG) (Lee et al 2016).   
In addition to stimulating myogenic responses, FUS application to the motor cortex has been shown to increase cortical activity in the target region through BOLD fMRI recordings (Yoo et al 2011). Of note, while the FUS-induced motor impulses were achieved with stimulation intensities of 12W/cm2 Isppa, the BOLD fMRI measurements reported activity in the target region even when the FUS was delivered at lower acoustic intensities, down to 3.6W/cm2 Isppa. This highlights a limitation for using the motor cortex to report FUS neuromodulation. While an extremely useful method of establishing FUS parameters that effectively stimulate small cortical targets, myogenic responses represent a threshold of FUS induced activity necessary to produce a motor impulse. This demonstrates only the immediate effect of FUS on the target tissue and the response may represent a combination of subthreshold changes at the cellular and network level. These aspects of FUS’s effect on cortical activity *in vivo* have been appreciated by monitoring experimentally evoked activity of cortical regions before and after FUS application to the region of interest. 

***FUS to subcortical and cortical targets modulates sensory evoked potentials***   
An important distinction should be made about the stimulatory vs modulatory effects of FUS. FUS can be used as a stimulus to induce evoked potentials, similar to the potentials induced by  somatosensory stimuli.  FUS was shown to induce activity in the visual cortex of sheep. This was measured as an evoked potential from electrodes placed near the visual cortex in the same way that one would measure VEPs after stimulating the animal with flashing lights (Lee et al 2016). However, in the studies covered in this section, the lasting neuromodulatory effects of FUS are measured by examining changes in the response of brain regions to their appropriate stimuli after the application of ultrasound. The stimuli in these cases are not the FUS pulses, but are instead either visual stimuli such as a flashing light or auditory stimuli such as tone pips played from a speaker near an animal's ear. While stimulating activity with the FUS itself could one day serve as a powerful non-invasive tool for mapping the function of cortical and subcortical brain regions even in humans, it is important to understand the lasting effects of sonication at useful parameters to inform the interpretation of results as well as assure the safety of human subjects. In regards to therapeutic and medical use, the lasting effects of FUS are promising findings that warrant further study to establish the exact parameters necessary to enhance or suppress target brain regions for extended periods of time.  	  
As discussed earlier, the first demonstration of FUS neuromodulation in the CNS was performed with an *in vivo* set-up and showed that FUS targeted to the LGN induced lasting but reversible suppression of visually evoked potentials (Fry et al 1958). Since then, the majority of evidence showing a lasting neuromodulatory effect after FUS has come from studies that used similar techniques to measure sensory evoked activity before and after FUS treatment. The neural substrates that could explain this lasting change have also been investigated and will be discussed in the next section.   
	FUS was shown to cause suppression of visually evoked potentials in rabbits and rats for a short time following sonication (Yoo et al 2011, Kim et al 2015). The suppressive effect on the VEP amplitude persisted for approximately 7 minutes following sonication and the reduced activity of the sonicated target region was also appreciated using BOLD fMRI measurements (Yoo et al 2011). Experiments using slightly higher levels of intensity and duty cycle showed that FUS could induce the opposite effect, causing an increase in the measured amplitude of VEPs both during and after FUS application (Kim et al 2015).These studies support the bimodal effect of FUS on targeted brain regions reported in previous *in vitro* investigations of FUS neuromodulation in peripheral nerves, hippocampal slices, and cell cultures (Rinaldi et al 1991, Bachtold et al 1998, Fry et al 1950, Lele et al 1963, Colucci et al 2009, Foley et al 2006, Tsui et al 2005). They provide examples of FUS induced changes in cortical behavior indicating that intensity and duty cycle are key FUS parameters that can influence the direction of neuromodulation.   
	FUS targeted to subdivisions of the thalamus in pigs resulted in suppression of SEPs triggered in nerves specific to the thalamic target (Dallapiaza et al 2018). When the ventroposterolateral nucleus (VPL) was targeted, the SEP induced by stimulating the tibial nerve was suppressed, with no effect on the SEPs induced by stimulating the trigeminal nerve. When the ventral posteromedial nucleus (VPN) was targeted, SEPs from trigeminal stimulation were suppressed with no effect on the SEPs from tibial stimulation. This selective suppression of targeted subcortical brain regions lasted approximately 10 minutes. Longer periods of suppression were found when rats were treated with FUS at the inferior colliculus and when pigs were treated with FUS at the auditory cortex (Daniels et al 2018). FUS delivered to both inferior colliculus and auditory cortex resulted in auditory evoked potential (AEP) suppression lasting from 30 minutes to multiple days, with one instance of month-long suppression before recovery. Increasing FUS intensity resulted in stronger suppression, providing another example of intensity’s role in determining neuromodulatory effects.    
	Finally, previous experiments from our lab provided evidence of FUS induced changes in sensory evoked response latency in mice electrically stimulated at the forelimb (Fisher and Gomenchuk 2018). When sensory stimuli were preceded by 1 second of low intensity FUS (0.69W/cm2, 50% DC) delivered to the somatosensory cortex, cortical responses (reported by voltage sensitive dye) occurred 3.0±0.7ms earlier compared to baseline. These effects were abolished with the application of TTX to the cortical region of interest..  
	Together, these studies provide examples of lasting FUS neuromodulation in live animal models, detected with measurements of sensory evoked activity. The lasting nature of modulation found in some of these experiments begs the question of what neurochemical changes, if any, are occuring to account for the maintained change in neural behavior following FUS application. 

***FUS induces changes in neurotrophic factors, neurotransmitters, and cellular metabolism***  
Previous sections have covered evidence of FUS induced neuromodulation in the form of cortical activity measurements, often through recordings of changes in electrode potentials. However, concurrent studies have sought to assess the FUS induced changes, if any, in the concentration of neurochemical substrates such as neurotrophic factors, neurotransmitters, and molecular measures of cell metabolism in target nervous tissue.   
	FUS stimulation of the hippocampus in anesthetized mice resulted in increased expression of brain derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) in CA1 and CA3 (Tufail et al 2010). Similar results were found when individual hemispheres were targeted broadly with FUS in a rat model (Yang et al 2015). Yang and colleagues not only found increased BDNF expression in the target hemisphere but also found increased glial derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF) expression. This result highlights the potential for FUS mediated neuromodulation to be mediated not only by neurons but also via astrocytes and other supportive glial cells. This increase in BDNF expression was shown to be mediated by increased calcium levels and activation of NF-kB by preventing FUS induced BDNF increase with a calcium chelator and inhibitor of NF-kB (Liu et al 2016).   
	The effects of FUS targeted to subcortical structures like LGN, Inferior Colliculus, VPL, and others has been measured with recordings of evoked potentials not only the structures targeted directly with FUS but in connected cortical structures as well. Such is the case for the suppression of VEPs and AEPs seen after targeting Inferior Colliculus with FUS (Fry et al 1958, Daniels et al 2019). The effect of FUS on connected structures has also been shown through measurements of neurotransmitter concentration in the frontal lobe after FUS stimulation of the thalamus. FUS to the thalamus resulted in increased levels of extracellular dopamine (DA) and serotonin (5-HT) in the frontal lobe, as well as decreased levels of extracellular gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) (Min et al 2011, Yang et al 2012).   
	Finally, levels of cellular metabolism were monitored with positron emission tomography (PET) imaging before and after FUS application to the target region. It was found that glucose uptake was increased within the targeted region of rat thalamus, however, this effect did not span the entire acoustic focus (Kim et al 2014). The acoustic focus of FUS is often defined as the region that experiences 50% of the maximum intensity of the FUS waves, with the very center of the focus having 100% intensity and a bell shaped drop in intensity at points further from the center (Dell’Italia et al 2022). The affected region showing increased glucose uptake did not span the width of the acoustic focus at half-maximum, but rather extended only across the region that would experience 90% of the maximum FUS intensity- a smaller region close to the very center of the FUS intensity field (Kim et al 2014). 

**Chapter 2: Parameters and General Instrumentation for Focused Ultrasonic Neuromodulation**  
***Overview***  
Establishing the parameters that determine the nature of ultrasound induced neuromodulation is paramount to making FUS’s use as a therapeutic and research tool a reality. Efforts to consolidate findings from animal and human studies have implicated two parameters, spatial peak pulse average intensity (Isppa) and duty cycle (DC), as key contributors to the direction of the change in activity (Baek et al. 2016, Blackmore et al. 2018, Wang et al. 2020). To avoid damage of target structures, recent studies have investigated parameters that avoid inducing changes via a thermal mechanism, instead using low intensities that are proposed to act through mechanical interactions with neural membranes and ion channels.  
*Put all mechanism-related stuff here. I’ve flagged stuff that I think can be brought into this section. I would give an overview few sentences and then categorize and describe background refs (e.g., thermal, membrane biomechanics, direct perturbation of ion channels, etc. I would also include in the ion channel stuff, which should be toward the end of this mechanism review, brief mention of the field of sonogenetics.* 

***Underlying Mechanisms***   
Ultrasound can be used to induce, enhance, and suppress activity through both thermal and mechanical mechanisms (Dell’Italia et al 2022, Plaskin et al 2016). The exact mechanism behind FUS induced neuromodulation is debated and may involve a combination of various proposed mechanisms with different contributions depending on the parameters of the FUS. The pressure waves of ultrasound, if delivered at sufficiently high intensity, can result in energy deposition in the form of thermal energy. This is the principle behind high intensity FUS (HFUS) used to induce lesions in disease targets in the CNS and PNS (Haar et al 2009). Early studies propose that changes in neural behavior after low-intensity FUS application to the target brain tissue also arise as a result of similar thermal effects (Lele et al 1963, Tsui et al 2005, Foley et al 2008, Colucci et al 2009). However, others support non-thermal mechanisms with reports of FUS induced neuromodulation accompanied by only minute temperature changes ranging from 0.002C to 0.3C measured in the target tissue (Yoo et al 2011, Lee et al 2016). Such temperature changes are below the threshold argued to be necessary for thermal stimulation/suppression of neural activity (Mihran et al 1990, Tufail et al 2010, Khraiche et al 2008, Plaskin et al 2016). The alternative mechanisms include the mechanical deformation of neural membranes resulting in changes in membrane capacitance, increases in membrane permeability through microcavitation/sonoporation, and the direct activation of mechanosensitive ion channels (Heimburg et al 2012, Plaskin et al 2014, Mihran et al. 1990, Tyler et al. 2008, Kubanek et al. 2016). The increasing number of studies on mechanosensitive ion channel subtypes expressed in the CNS has provided ample evidence supporting their pivotal role in the neuromodulation induced by FUS. Subtypes of mechanosensitive ion channels including TRPP1/2, TRPC1, and Piezo1 have been shown to be responsive to ultrasound mechanical forces and necessary in some cases to produce FUS neuromodulation (Yoo et al 2022, Duque et al 2022, Zhu et al 2023). Finally, the controlled expression of mechanically sensitive channels in neural targets has allowed for the emergence of the field of ‘sonogenetics” in which ultrasound fulfills the role of the illuminating light used in optogenetic neuromodulation to stimulate optically sensitive ion channels (Isben et al. 2015, Kubanek et al 2018, Soloperto et al 2018, Wang et al 2020). 

***Instrumentation***   
The ultrasonic waves applied to neural tissue in FUS neuromodulation experiments are delivered from an ultrasound transducer, which can be made of single or multiple elements. These elements produce pressure waves with frequencies in the ultrasonic range (typically at least 100kHz) that focus to a single point at which the highest acoustic intensity occurs. Around this point extends a region where acoustic intensities decrease further from the exact center. Typically, when studies refer to the “acoustic focus” of FUS, they are referring to the region of space that experiences 50% of the maximum pressure changes or “full-width, half-maximum”. When measuring relative pressure, this is defined as the space experiencing down to \-6dB points pressure squared. The ultrasound transducer we use in our experiments, for example, has a focal width of 504μm (H-405, Sonic Concepts, Inc., Bothell, WA). 

***Stimulus Parameter Design***  
	FUS can be delivered continuously or with repeated pulses of ultrasonic bursts. When FUS is given in bursts, the acoustic intensity at the ultrasound focus is often reported as either spatial peak pulse average intensity (Isppa) or spatial peak temporal average intensity (Ispta). They measure the average intensity delivered at the center of the acoustic focus over a single burst, or averaged over the period of time from the start of one pulse to the start of the next pulse. In the latter case, the “silence” in between the bursts are taken into account.   
![][image1]  
The ratio of the time elapsed over one FUS burst (burst length, BL) and the time in between each pulse (pulse repetition period, PRP) is called the duty cycle (DC). This measure is commonly used to report the percentage of time during a pulsed FUS sonication that ultrasonic waves were actually producing pressure changes at the focus. For example, while a continuous FUS stimulation would have a DC of 100%, our experiments employed a DC of 50% since the bursts of FUS we used were 5ms in length, while the time between the start of each was 10ms in length.   
The frequencies used in FUS neuromodulation range from 100kHz to several MHz. While higher frequencies allow for higher focusing power and higher spatial resolution, current studies on low intensity FUS induced neuromodulation tend to use frequencies below 1MHz because lower frequency ultrasonic waves generally have better penetration through skull and tissue. Our transducer uses 750kHz as its fundamental frequency. 

**Chapter 3: Random-access targeted ultrasound neuromodulation combined with widefield Ca2+ imaging *in vivo***  
***Window material***   
Though contested, prior studies in which FUS was applied across thinned-skull have proposed that resonance of the FUS waves within the skull served as a source of off-target stimulation of auditory pathways (Guo et al 2018, Sato et al 2018, Mohammadjavadi et al 2019). Furthermore, skull tissue has been shown to attenuate ultrasonic waves passing through the bone and reduce their intensity (Guo et al 2022). We sought to navigate these issues by performing a full craniotomy (rather than thinning the bone) and using polymethylpentene as a material for the window. This material is transparent visually and acoustically, providing minimal attenuation to the FUS waves and preventing resonance forming with bone (Koekkoek et al 2018). As stated in other sections, the FUS sonication treatment is not the stimulation we used in our experiments. It occurs as a singular event in between our pre-FUS and post-FUS calcium imaging trials. Our stimulus during each trial is a pure-tone chirp triggered to play from a speaker that plays these pure-tones directly into the mouse’s ear through a connected tube. Any resonance-acoustics during FUS sonication could not impact the responses to our auditory stimuli in subsequent trials because the FUS is never turned on *during* any trials. 

***Ultrasound equipment and parameters***  
We use a custom built, phase array, 16-element steerable FUS transducer built by Sonic Concepts (H-405, Sonic Concepts, Inc., Bothell, WA). All experiments used the fundamental frequency for sonications. The focal coordinates of the X and Y plane were altered for every experiment to target the active regions identified in calcium images from pre-treatment trials.

| Parameter | Settings |
| :---: | :---: |
| Fundamental Frequency | 750kHz |
| Intensity (Isppa) | Calcium-imaging: 0.6W/cm2; 1.2W/cm2 Thermochrome: \>200W/cm2 IHC/Evans blue: \>200W/cm2 |
| Burst Length | Calcium-imaging: 5ms Thermochrome: 10ms IHC/Evans blue: 10ms |
| Pulse Repetition Period | All: 10ms |
| Duty Cycle | Calcium-imaging: 50% Thermochrome: 100% (Continuous) IHC/Evans blue: 100% (Continuous) |
| Treatment Duration | Calcium-imaging: 20s Thermochrome: 1-5s IHC/Evans blue: 20s |

Table 1: Ultrasound parameters used across different experiments.

Fig.\* Schematic of custom transducer. Images were taken through the transducer’s aperture which did not change the camera’s field of view (FOV). Targets were specified by choosing pixel coordinates from images taken at this FOV.

***Sonication***  
We used a Matlab interface to specify frequency, X and Y coordinates, electrical power per transducer channel, burst length, burst period, and treatment duration. X and Y coordinates differed from experiment to experiment and were chosen manually based on the most responsive regions from the pre-sonication imaging portion of each experiment. X coordinate inputs were corrected for an offset of \-0.23mm (identified with thermochromic-sheet and hydrophone assessments described later in this chapter). All calcium imaging experiments used a burst length of 5ms and a pulse repetition period of 10ms. This resulted in a duty cycle of 50% and a pulse repetition frequency of 100Hz. All experiments used a treatment duration of 20 seconds. *Mice were sonicated at either 0.625W/cm2 or 1.2W/cm2 Isppa.*   
![][image2]  
Fig.\* Schematic of targeting procedure for shifting sonication focus. Schematic shows all elements of the transducer in phase with each other to target the center of the FOV. Matlab interfaces were used to input target coordinates and calculate the necessary alterations in the phase of each of the transducer’s 16 elements. Other parameters set in this interface included electrical power (determining FUS intensity at the focus), burst length and period (determining duty cycle), and treatment duration (20 seconds in all cases). 

***Thermochromic sheet experiments: overview***  
We developed an in-situ method of assessing the targeting capabilities of our custom 16-element transducer by targeting multiple points on thermochromic sheets. In addition to using a novel combination of imaging and targeting FUS in neuromodulation experiments, we wanted to provide a method of assessing targeting accuracy within the same apparatus. Thermochromic sheets are thin films embedded with crystals that change color when a temperature threshold is met. We used our transducer’s ability to produce high intensities of FUS which can elicit heat at the focus.  These intensities were much higher than those used during calcium imaging experiments where the goal was neuromodulation without thermal effects. Using these properties of thermochrome and high intensity FUS, we were able to visualize the location of the ultrasound focus as it was targeted to various locations within the field of view. 

***Thermochromic sheet experiments: methods***  
To visualize heating at the FUS focus, we needed to use a camera that could capture color images since the thermochromic crystals report heat via changes in color.  The CMOS camera we used for calcium-imaging experiments was only capable of capturing black and white images. We instead used a DS-F*i*3 camera mounted on the same imaging apparatus used for fluorescence imaging (NIKON Corp.,Tokyo, Japan). The key challenge posed by this camera change was a difference in the focal lengths of the two cameras. Our ultrasound transducer was designed such that the focus of FUS (2.82mm from the exit plane of the transducer housing) would be at the optical focus of the microscope when our CMOS camera was equipped. The NIKON color camera required the objective to be closer to the imaged object for a focused image. As a result, the FUS delivered to the thermochrome during these experiments had focal areas larger than the true geometric focus dimensions. Ultrasonic focussing occurs in an hour-glass shape, such that at the exact geometric focus, the waves of FUS impact the smallest area. In our experiments with the NIKON camera equipped, the plane of the sonicated region was closer to the transducer than normal, resulting in a larger area experiencing the sonication. While this is important to note, it did not prevent us from determining the zero point of our transducer or from calculating any adjustments necessary to properly target locations within the field of view. Our typical lighting solution (an ark-light filtered through prisms to produce desired wavelengths for fluorescent reporters) oversaturated the color-camera and prevented us from detecting any appreciable color change. As a result, I used a separately mounted light source with adjustable arms to light the sheet from two angles. Two thermochromic sheets of differing response thresholds were tested for their ability to report temperature changes. These low and high thresholds both spanned 5°C and were 30-35°C and 35°-40°C respectively. They were submerged under de-gassed water in a petri-dish and secured using strips of tape.   
![][image3]  
Fig.\*: Thermochromic sheet experiment diagram and sample color image of a sonicated target. FUS was applied to sheets without any targeting parameters (x:0,y:0) to assess any offset in the sonication focus. Multiple trials were averaged to determine a 0.23mm offset in the x plane, and all target coordinates for future experiments were adjusted accordingly. 

***Higher Temperature Threshold Sheets are Superior for Targeting***    
While the low threshold sheets were more sensitive to FUS sonications, they proved difficult to use due to saturation. During sonication, the surrounding water was consistently heated beyond the sensitivity threshold (30-35°C) and did not cool sufficiently below the threshold in between applications of the continuous wave, high intensity FUS. The high intensity FUS with 100% DC settings was used exclusively during these experiments, and was intended to heat the target material at the acoustic focus. However, if the duration of each sonication event exceeded approximately 5-10 seconds, the continuous ultrasonic waves within the fluid sack would transfer heat throughout the water contained by the fluid filled sac, as well as the water bath in which the thermochromic sheets were submerged. This occurred during most calibration experiments. However, the high-threshold sheets were much more resistant to saturation. As previously stated, the true focus of the FUS waves was positioned slightly below the plane of the thermochrome sheets. In addition to increasing the size of the focus experienced by the sheet, the intensities at this plane were lower than those at the true focus. As a result, we needed to apply more intense and longer FUS sonications to induce thermal changes at the plane of the thermochromic sheet, which contributed to the heating of the degassed water. Future experiments could improve on this with the use of a single camera capable of capturing calcium fluorescence images as well as color images, thereby avoiding issues due to different focal lengths. 

***Thermochromic sheet experiments: image processing***  
Image J was used to identify centroids, calculate coordinates of the center of mass of each centroid, and average over multiple sonications at the same coordinates to provide the true zero point of our transducer. No offset was found in the Y axis but we did see a slight rightward shift of the focus in all targeted locations. We compensated for this offset in the X axis (-0.23mm to all X coordinates) in all experiments involving targeted FUS. We confirmed the targeting capabilities of the transducer by accounting for this offset in our target coordinates and observing the color change in the intended target locations on the thermochromic sheets (Fig.\*, A, B, C).   
![][image4]  
***Hydrophone calibration***  
We performed a calibration using a hydrophone (with the help of collaborators at the Konofugau lab at Columbia) to characterize our transducer’s ultrasound focus both axially and horizontally. This required a large tank with degassed water and a high-sensitivity bullet hydrophone that could report pressure readings at various locations in the focal plane of the submerged transducer. The axial protocol involved moving the hydrophone stepwise towards the transducer (in the z direction) across a predetermined length of 7 inches. The starting point would adjust horizontally one pixel length in the y direction as soon as the whole axial length had been covered once. The horizontal protocol required the tip of the hydrophone to move in the x and y direction, but with no change in the distance from the transducer (no z movement).

Fig.\*: Hydrophone calibration setup. A) Bullet hydrophone recording voltage readings (converted to pressure in MPa). The hydrophone was mounted to a manipulator arm to change the hydrophone’s position in x, y, and z axes. B) 16-Element, Scanning FUS Transducer positioned such that the FUS focus was directed towards the hydrophone. C) Transducer controller box for setting parameters and triggering FUS pulses. 

 The focal point of the transducer was identified by the greatest pressure change induced when no phase changes were input and all elements of the transducer were in phase. The values we obtained were in line with initial specifications provided by the transducer manufacturer’s hydrophone test report. This said, we thoroughly tested small variations of input parameters that were not delineated in the initial calibration profile to better characterize the transducer at the intensity levels we used for neuromodulation.  
Results from this calibration confirmed the previously identified focus-offset in the X axis from the thermochromic sheet experiments detailed in the previous section. The focus identified during beam-mapping was located 0.23mm away from the center of the transducer’s aperture in the positive direction on the X axis, and directly in line with the 0 point of the Y axis. This calibration defined the experimental parameters we used in neuromodulation experiments. With this calibration, we could reliably induce specific pressure values (and thus specific FUS intensities measured in Isppa) at the target by giving the necessary electrical wattage inputs to each of the transducer’s 16 channels.

Fig\*: Horizontal (left) and axial (right) profiles of pressures at the ultrasound focus measured with a hydrophone in a degassed water tank. Results match the transducer manufacturer’s specifications with a focal width of \~0.5mm, a focal length of 3.1mm, and a depth of focus of 2.82mm. 

***Wide-field calcium imaging***  
One of the limitations of existing literature reporting the *in vivo* effects of FUS is that the measurements of neural activity have very low spatial resolution. In some cases, these measurements have no spatial resolution, such as reports of a rapid recovery from anesthesia following FUS stimulation or the activation of muscle twitches after sonication of primary motor cortex (Tufail et al 2010, King et al 2013, King et al 2014, Mehic et al 2014, Younan et al 2013, Kim et al 2014, Kim et al 2014, Yoo et al 2011, Lee et al 2016). The reports of lasting change to FUS do sometimes include imaging techniques such as fMRI to locate active regions (Yoo et al 2011). However, much of the evidence supporting a long-term change in neural behavior following exposure to FUS comes in either reports of changes in neural substrate concentration (Tufail et al. 2010, Min et al. 2011, Yang et al. 2012, Kim et al. 2014, Yang et al. 2015, Liu et al. 2016\) or in electrode recordings of stimulus-evoked potentials (Fry et al 2958, Yoo et al 2011, Daniels et al 2019). Neither methodology examines the cortex with spatial resolution on par with the FUS focus itself, which in our case spans a half millimeter. Furthermore, they provide little information on the propagation of neural activity across target cortical regions, since electrical recordings aggregate potential changes over large regions and are attenuated due to the skull overlying the activity (Guo et al 2022). 

***Choice of reporter***  
Wide-field fluorescence imaging, in comparison, dramatically improves the spatial resolution in measurements of cortical activity. There are a number of reporters to choose from for this kind of imaging. Each has benefits and constraints, primarily with regards to kinetics and signal-to-noise ratio. Prior work in this lab employed voltage sensitive dyes and took advantage of their rapid kinetics to study response timing in activity of primary sensory cortex in mice (Fisher and Gomenchuk 2018). This was possible because FUS effects were examined acutely. My experiments, however, require longitudinal study of cortical responses over multiple imaging sessions, which could not be done within the narrow use-window of voltage sensitive dyes. In addition, because we wanted  to characterize baseline levels of tonotopy in the auditory cortex, we needed clear and prominent signals. The signal to noise ratio of voltage sensitive dyes is very small and as a result, the spatial profile of the response appears jagged. Experiments using these dyes would require many trials to be averaged into an appreciable signal. From prior attempts conducted in our lab, this would require extensive smoothing of the jagged regions and complicate the identification of already coarse borders that define tonotopic regions in the auditory cortex. These regions have considerable overlap of tone-specific neural populations spanning 100-200µm (Merzenich et al 1973, Bandyopadhyay et al 2010, Hacket et al 2011, Guo et al 2012, Romero et al 2019).  
 Calcium reporters are another option for fluorescent imaging that can be used over a larger period of time for chronic imaging. Calcium reporters are widely used as correlates for neural activity and can either be directly introduced to desired cortex with viral vectors or expressed in genetically engineered mouse lines (Chen et al. 2013). Vector based expression has multiple draw-backs, such as requiring multiple injections to cover the \~3mm of cortex we could examine with a craniotomy and incomplete expression. The vector method of inserting calcium reporters into our cortical region of interest would at the very least introduce multiple sources for variability between experiments. Mouse lines avoid these hurdles, and those expressing the GCaMP6 group of reporters expressed under the Thy1 promoter have the added benefit of better performance compared to vector-based versions of the same reporter (Dana et al 2014). The low signal to noise ratio of other GCaMP subtypes such as GCaMP3 or GCaMP6f were undesirable for similar reasons to the voltage sensitive dyes. While available as mouse lines, they would still make it difficult to appreciate stimulus induced responses in a cortical environment with high variability, given the heavy influence of anesthesia on cortical propagation and tonotopy (Guo et al 2012, Stroh et al 2022). Because we planned to use isoflurane as our primary anesthetic agent (favoring the control of anesthetic depth that it provides over the variability it introduces), we chose a mouse line expressing the Thy1-GCaMP6s reporter as our mouse model. It has the highest signal to noise ratio among similar calcium reporters, has been used to observe tone-specific responses in the auditory cortex, and its response characteristics have been described in the context of isoflurane induced anesthesia (Romero et al 2019).

**Chapter 4: Widefield Ca2+ imaging in mouse auditory cortex**  
*Two sentences on why auditory for FUS*   
*ACx organization and subdivisions not necessarily so straightforward*  
*Our investigations enabled us to explore in greater depth ACx*   
*Some of the major knowledge gaps are, for instance…*  
***Overview***  
The highly conserved spatial organization of the auditory cortex provides a cortical region with populations of pitch-selective neurons that can serve as targets to test effects of FUS. Though subdivisions of ACx and the strength of sensory representation patterns vary somewhat with measurement techniques, anesthetic state, and animal model, general patterns can be appreciated in different portions of ACx during the response to tonal stimuli. The effects of FUS on the ACx have been investigated by a small number of studies that used low-spatial resolution techniques (AEP) to quantify changes in cortical activity (Daniels et al 2019).  As a result, the effect of FUS on precise locations of particularly active neural populations within ACx has yet to be investigated. Our approach enabled us to explore FUS neuromodulation of ACx in greater depth and appreciate the activity of tonally responsive neural populations at spatially distinct cortical locations. 

***The auditory system***  
![][image5]  
The auditory pathway begins at the outer ear. Pressure waves, such as those produced during human speech, are transmitted through the air and cause the tympanic membrane of the sensing ear to vibrate. A series of three connected bones (or ossicles) in the middle ear transmit these vibrations from the tympanic membrane to the oval window of the cochlea. The resulting movement of the oval window causes waves to propagate through fluid within the cochlea with higher or lower frequencies depending on how fast or slow (respectively) the tympanic membrane, ossicles, and oval window are vibrating.  
![][image6]  
 These waves of varying frequencies within the cochlear fluid in turn cause a long membrane within the cochlea to vibrate at different points along the cochlea’s length. The membrane is tapered, being thick at the base and flimsy at the apex. This means that the base is perturbed by waves with higher frequencies, while the apex is perturbed by waves with lower frequencies. Situated above this basilar membrane are the first neurons within the auditory pathway: the hair cells. These specialized cells have ion channels that are opened by the mechanical force provided by the basilar membrane underneath. They do not have axons, but synapse onto the axons of bipolar cells (the spiral ganglion within the cochlear nerve), which transmit afferent and efferent information to and from the more central parts of the auditory pathway.   
![][image7]  
The spatial separation of low and high frequencies at the cochlea, due to the properties of the basilar membrane, results in the first tonotopic gradient. Tonotopy refers to the spatial separation (“-topy”) of tones or sounds (“ton-”). As previously stated, this spatial separation of neural activation is maintained as the fibers of the spiral ganglion and the auditory nerve carry information to the brainstem (with fibers running to the cochlear nucleus, the superior olivary nucleus, and the inferior colliculus),  the medial geniculate body (MGB) of the thalamus, and finally the multiple subdivisions of the auditory cortex.   
Within the cortex, the extent of tonotopy has been investigated in a wide array of animal models and shows relatively consistent patterns of tonal representation (Merzenich et al 1973, Bandyopadhyay et al 2010, Hacket et al 2011, Guo et al 2012, Romero et al 2019). It should be noted that these patterns are more difficult to observe under certain states of anesthesia and when limiting measurements to individual neurons instead of larger groups of neurons spanning \~100-200µm of cortex (Rothschild et al 2010, Kanold et al 2014). Mapping studies have shown that in mouse primary auditory cortex (A1), low frequency tones cause activity at the caudal end while high frequency tones cause activity in the rostral end, producing a gradient that responds to frequencies from \~4kHz through \~64kHz tones. Interestingly, the adjacent auditory cortical region just rostral to A1, appropriately named the anterior auditory field (AAF) exhibits a mirror image tonal gradient to A1. It shows activity in response to high frequencies at its caudal border to A1, while lower frequencies produce activity in the most rostral  of AAF (Guo et al 2012). These core auditory cortical regions receive input from the MGB of the thalamus with the tonal gradient being maintained within the thalamo-cortical connecting fibers (Hacket et al 2011). The tonotopic organization is also preserved within columns spanning the 5 cortical layers of A1 and associated regions. Though these layers are made up of different cell types, reports show columns approximately 100µm wide, of neurons in L1, L2/3, L4, L5 and L6, that respond to the same preferred tones (Tischbirek et al 2019). There are some experimental factors that can influence the observed tonotopy such as the loudness of the sound stimulus in addition to the anesthetic state of the animal under study. Despite some degradation of tonotopy with loud sounds (\~60dB to 70dB) and with increasing depths of anesthesia, however, a coarse but appreciable tonotopy is still preserved and observable in the core auditory cortical regions A1 and AAF (Guo et al 2012).   
Auditory cortex provides a relatively unexplored testing ground for FUS neuromodulation with characteristics that can help us take advantage of the high spatial resolution possible in FUS. The tonotopic organization can provide a range of spatially distinct targets for ultrasound with the use of auditory stimuli of different frequencies. There are confounds to consider, however, to avoid issues with this type of stimulus.   
Mice can hear in the ultrasonic range, though the representation of frequencies in the core auditory cortices (A1 and AAF) is largely limited to sounds from 5kHz-64kHz (Romero et al 2019, Heffner et al 2007). That said, the frequency of ultrasound used in my experiments detailed in chapter 4 (750kHz)  is much higher than the maximum frequency that mice can hear (100kHz). Furthermore, as is stated previously and in later sections, our stimulus is *not* FUS. We use ultrasound as a treatment to compare the responses of mice to tone pips that they are exposed to before and after the FUS treatment. These acoustic stimuli are played through a separate speaker and are well below ultrasonic frequencies, only ranging from 10kHz to 30kHz.  Our measurements are made while the mouse hears audible sounds in their hearing range, not while the mouse is being sonicated with FUS. This also avoids a second potential confound, which is the reported activation of peripheral auditory pathways by FUS reverberations within the target animal’s skull (Guo et al 2018, Sato et al 2018\) although conflicting results call this off-target effect into question (Mohammadjavadi et al 2019). By measuring responses before and after a single FUS treatment, we are not at risk of attributing the stimulus of activity in the auditory cortex to FUS. Finally, we used a window material specially chosen for its lack of acoustic properties for our experiments involving FUS. To visualize and apply FUS directly to the mouse auditory cortex, we performed craniotomies and implanted transparent windows through which we could both capture images and sonicate target cortical regions. A window material such as glass or thinned bone causes attenuation of ultrasound passing through (Guo et al 2022). Furthermore, the interaction of the ultrasonic waves with these materials could contribute to off-target effects like activation of peripheral auditory pathways or increased thermal deposition due to reverberations of the skull (Constans et al 2017, Kamimura et al 2016). We use, however, polymethylpentene sheets, which are both visually and acoustically transparent (Koekkoek et al 2018). This material allows for minimal interference of the light and sound waves that pass through it, thereby preventing FUS from vibrating the skull as well as avoiding any attenuation of the ultrasonic focus. 

***Breeding strategy to rescue hearing loss phenotype***  
Before using Thy1-GCaMP6s mice for calcium imaging of the auditory cortex, we needed to address a key issue with the available mouse line’s phenotype. The background of the mouse line was C57B6/J which has one of the worst hearing profiles of the mouse strains available (Zheng et al 1999, Jones et al 2006). The hearing loss in these mice occurs due to a defect in a gene that encodes for an important structural protein within the inner hair cells of the cochlea. Lacking the correctly functioning protein, these hair cells are extremely prone to damage and degrade rapidly over time. Without properly functioning hair cells, the acoustic stimuli we planned to use would not induce a sensory signal from the cochlea and the primary auditory cortex would not receive stimulation. To rescue this phenotype, prior studies have crossed their C57B6/J mice with a mouse strain primarily used in  studies of diabetes: CBA/Caj (Romero et al 2019). CBA/Caj  mice have the best hearing profile of available mouse lines, and when crossed with C57B6/J mice, provide the progeny with a working allele for the inner-hair cell protein (Jones et al 2006). As a result, hair cell integrity is maintained and hearing persists past juvenile ages. Given this, we crossed CBA/Caj mice with our Thy1-GCaMP6s mice to form our complete mouse model. Presence of the calcium reporter in male progeny was confirmed with genotyping after weaning (Genotyping was performed by Transnetyx using real time PCR. 

***ABR testing***  
We used auditory brainstem response (ABR) testing to establish the hearing profiles of our mice prior to and after surgery. The results from these tests demonstrate normal hearing in our mice. Most importantly, they confirm the ability for each mouse that was used in calcium imaging experiments to hear a 70dB tone of either 10kHz or 30kHz in frequency (the frequencies used for auditory stimuli).  

Fig.\*: ABR recording setup and processing software.   
We used the MF1 speaker, RZ6 processing unit, Medusa amplifier, and BioSig software to conduct these tests (TDT, Tucker Davis Technologies). Mice were anesthetized with isoflurane and buprenorphine as described in the methods subsection on surgical procedures. Electrodes were placed subcutaneously according to (Fig.\*) with the reference electrode positioned midline above the brainstem, the primary channel electrode positioned either just medial or temporo-lateral to the ipsilateral ear receiving the auditory stimulus, and the ground electrode inserted SQ on the proximal part of either hind leg. Importantly, electrodes placed such that only their tips reached these locations would NOT produce an appreciable ABR reading. The middle and base of these electrodes needed to be underneath the skin and located as close to the underlying brainstem/ ipsilateral cochlea as possible to record the propagation of sensory evoked voltage changes.   
![][image8]  
Mice were presented with 512 chirps of the same auditory stimulus to create one averaged ABR trace. Tones were presented at frequencies starting from 5kHz up to 33kHz in steps of 3kHz. For each frequency, 512 chirps were played at each sound level, starting from the loudest 80dB and ending at 20dB with decreasing steps of 10dB. ABR curves were filtered with a lowpass filter of 500Hz and a high-pass filter of 3kHz before being evaluated for the presence of characteristic peaks/wave components. For the evaluation of hearing threshold, the sound level threshold of each frequency was defined as the lowest sound level (dB) that produced a recognizable ABR curve. 

***Challenges in ABR recording***   
ABR recordings were often difficult to acquire because of interference from electromagnetic waves (our noise floor was high). To address this, we constructed a custom built faraday cage using wood, copper mesh, and a grounding wire. Wood planks were cut and sanded down to size to make a 17” by 13” open box frame. Copper mesh was wrapped and joined to this frame such that all sides were completely shielded with copper. The mesh was connected with a grounding wire to the bench top, and continuity was confirmed using a multimeter voltmeter. With this custom faraday cage, ABR curves were more easily detectable, especially at the low end of the mouse hearing range. 

Fig.\* Schematic of faraday cage used to block interfering electromagnetic waves.   
Another challenge with ABR recording was that proper placement of the recording electrodes was impeded after surgery and cranial window placement due to the presence of acrylic on the scalp. In a subset of mice, the surgical procedure was slightly modified to minimize the amount of dorsal scalp exposed. By removing less skin overlying the caudal aspect of the scalp, less acrylic was necessary. These modified surgeries were intended to allow post-operative electrode recordings for both ABR threshold measurements and AEP recordings pre and post FUS. However, we were unable to reliably record clean ABR curves during sonication experiments. Future experiments could improve on the surgical approach, or employ embedded screws in the skull as contact points for electrodes. This would add another measurement of the auditory sensory pathway to evaluate changes, if any, after FUS application to primary auditory cortex. 

***Surgical and imaging apparatus***   
The temporal aspect of the mouse head needed to be presented flat (parallel to the ground) at the imaging focus of our microscope. The head needs to be rotated around the long axis of the mouse body and rigidly held in place to prevent motion artifacts. This obviates the use of traditional stereotactic apparatuses with contact points on both sides of the skull. To achieve rotation and secure positioning, I used a ball and socket mounted manipulator arm that connects to a metal bar fixed to the dorsal surface of the skull with acrylic.  
![][image9]  
Fig.\*: Custom mounting apparatus showing experimental positioning of mouse head relative to nose-cone (orange securing tape) which was used to provide isoflurane anesthesia during surgery and imaging. Top Inset: Schematic showing positioning of head-mount relative to the transverse suture. Head-mounts were always positioned extending to the contralateral side of the craniotomy window (red outline). Dental acrylic covered any exposed skull (blue shading). Bottom inset: Custom extensor-arm screwed into TRB2 socket joint mounted at 90 degrees. 

***Individual trials***  
Individual trials consisted of synchronized events triggered by LabView software.   
First, the shutter for the halogen lamp is triggered open to illuminate the craniotomy (Fig.\*, A, Green line). The CMOS camera is then triggered to begin capturing a series of 100 images (frames) at 18.57fps (Fig.\*, B, Blue line). Three seconds after the start of the image capture trigger, prior to the 56th frame, the acoustic stimulus is triggered to play once from the MF1 speaker connected to the mouse’s left ear (Fig.\*,C, Red line). After all 100 images have been captured (5.38 seconds after the first image capture trigger), the shutter is closed and the trial ends (Fig.\*,D, Green line). 

Fig.\*:Synchrony diagram of individual calcium imaging trials. **Green line:** trace of fluorescent light shutter being **(A)** opened and  **(D)** closed at the start and end of each trial. **Blue line:** trace of 100 image captures (frames) taken by the CMOS camera during a trial (not all frames represented in diagram). **Red line:** trace of auditory stimulus playing a single tone chirp **(C)** 3 seconds after the start of the first image capture **(B)**. 

Trials were executed automatically in groups of 10 for pre-treatment imaging to capture baseline activity prior to treatment (See Experimental Outline Diagram). After treatment, trials were executed in groups of either 30 (if single tone experiment) or 40 (if dual tone experiment, with 20 trials per tone). For all groups of trials, the amount of time between the end of one trial and the start of the next (the inter-trial delay) was 51 seconds. 

***Spatial characteristics of cortical responses to auditory stimuli***  
In a subset of mice (n=2), frequencies from across the hearing range confirmed with ABR testing were presented without a treatment phase to determine the extent of tonotopy capable of being visualized with our Thy1-GCaMP6s reporter.  Mice were presented with tones of 5, 10, 15, and 25kHz to elicit spatially distinct responsive regions in primary auditory cortex. The responsive regions were compared at the moment of peak intensity. We observed considerable overlap of the responsive regions to these different frequencies as reported by other studies in mice under anesthesia and with stimuli above activation threshold (\>60dB). However, when thresholded to the 15% most responsive pixels, the separate regions of activity captured in response to distinct tones could be coarsely visualized (Fig.\* Right).   
![][image10]  
Fig.\*: Tonotopic representation in A1. Left: Calcium responses to 5 and 10kHz  tones.
