Right: (separate mouse) Calcium responses to 5, 10, 15, and 20kHz tones overlaid. Though overlapping, the separate regions can be appreciated. The gradual shift from caudal to rostral regions with low to high frequencies matches with descriptions of tonotopy in A1.  

Across later calcium imaging experiments, a distinct spatio-temporal response pattern arose in response to repeated 10kHz tone pips. Activity would arise in a central location within the craniotomy-window FOV before shifting caudally to a region approximately 1-1.2mm away. These regions are separated by the rhinal vein. 

Fig\* Movement of auditory evoked activity measured as dF/F over the course of the cortical response. Left: Source (right circle) and sink (left circle) are 1.2mm apart. Right: activity starts at location rostral to the rhinal vein. (Both images taken from Mouse H1). 

The “source” of activity appears in a region that corresponds to core auditory cortices, primarily A1 and AAF. On initial impressions of the activity movement, we considered that the source of activity was AAF and that this activity moved to the caudally situated A1. The frequency used during these experiments, 10kHz, is on the lower end of the frequency range represented in both AAF and A1. As a result, such a tone would induce activity at the polar ends of these two regions, which have mirror-image tonotopic gradients. According to a study by Hacket and colleagues in 2011, these auditory sub-regions with best frequencies near 10kHz would be separated by approximately 1-1.25mm, nearly identical to the measured distance in our images. Furthermore, the vascular landmarks in images captured during our experiments match up with the landmarks appreciated by a 2019 study by Romero and colleagues.

Fig\* Comparison of our imaging location with prior experiments demonstrating tonotopy of A1 and AAF. Left: Figure from Romero et al 2019 showing A1 and AAF with mirror tonotopic organization. Right: Image taken from our experiments (mouse H1) in which calcium activity begins in the location marked “a” and travels to “b” over \~260 ms.   
   
While the orientation of AAF and A1 and the distance of the locations in both regions that respond to low frequency tones near 10kHz support AAF and A1 as the identities of the “source” and “sink” seen in our experiments, the time-course of the observed movement is suspect. Core auditory regions receive thalamic input from MGB in parallel rather than as a hierarchical pathway from thalamus to one cortical region to the next. The delay of activity from thalamus to either A1 or AAF would be on the order of 10ms. In our experiments, the source appears \~3-5 frames (\~160-260 ms) after stimulus onset while the sink appears another 3-5frames later. While the initial delay can be attributed to the kinetics of calcium fluctuations and calcium reporter conformational changes, the second delay should not be expected for parallel thalamic inputs. It may be that the anesthetic state of the mouse contributes to changes in thalamo-cortical processing such that a delay to these auditory regions could arise (Stroh et al 2022).   
However, a secondary explanation is that one of several secondary auditory cortices may be the sink we see in our experiments, while the source is A1. Potential identities for this caudally and ventrally situated region are ventral posterior auditory field (VPAF) and temporal association cortex (TEa) (Allen Brain Atlas, Feigin et al 2021, Romero et al 2019, Luo et al 2022). TEa in particular is supported by the observation that during the initial portion of imaging trials, caudal regions near our “sink” would activate in response to the visual stimuli provided by the fluorescent excitation light. Regardless of exact identity, the regions imaged during calcium imaging experiments were always responsive to tonal stimuli and the responses were timely, occurring \~160-260 ms after the onset of the acoustic stimulus. 

***Measures of cortical activity***  
We stimulated each mouse with an auditory stimulus and recorded the 2.6mm wide region of cortex to measure the changes in fluorescence indicating activity within the population of neurons visible in that FOV.   
Since we are imaging a large number of cells in wide-field, the fluorescent signal we measure will be averaged over the entire span of imaged cortex or over a smaller region of interest (ROI) within the FOV. The fractional fluorescence signal is calculated as dF/F, which is the change in fluorescent intensity over baseline fluorescence. This value is calculated for each pixel in all 100 frames of each video we capture, with each frame consisting of 800x800 pixels. The four frames that precede stimulus onset are used as the baseline. While we could average over the entire image (resulting in a measurement of calcium-reported activity similar to that obtained by fiber-optic photometry) we instead used a threshold of \>90%dF/F to identify the subregion of the imaged cortex whose fluorescence increased the strongest in response to the auditory stimulus.  This subregion of maximally responsive cortex provided the fluorescent signal that we averaged to create a trace of intensity over time. It is also the region that we targeted with FUS during the treatment phase in between the pre-treatment and post-treatment imaging phases (See Experimental Group Schematic).   
Rather than defining this region using a single response prior to FUS treatment, we used  the average of all pre-treatment imaging trials. We averaged these images taken during the pre-treatment imaging trials, after which we then applied the \>90%dF/F threshold. The resulting region of interest was defined for each individual mouse. This highly-responsive ROI for a given mouse was used as a mask to analyze the same cortical target for every trial within that mouse’s experiment. By processing the fluorescence changes in this tonally-responsive region across all trials, we produced individual traces of dF/F over time for both the pre-treatment and post-treatment trials. This provided a consistent subset of cortical area for comparing changes due to FUS, since the center of this subregion was used as our target coordinates for FUS sonication during the treatment phase. We therefore created traces that represent the activity of the neurons we intended to modulate (Fig 3 B and C).  
The following sections will often refer to cortical activity as reported by the calcium indicator expressed by our transgenic mouse line. It should be noted that calcium concentration is an indirect indicator of neural activity and does not exactly reflect other measures of neuronal function like rate of AP firing, changes in membrane voltage, etc. Furthermore, the GCaMP6s calcium reporter has distinct kinetics that further limit its temporal representation of neuronal activity (Chen et al 2013, Dana et al 2014). Lastly, the Thy1 promoter limits the expression of the reporter to L2/3 and L5 projection (pyramidal) neurons, meaning any reported fluorescence reflects changes in only a subset of the cortical cell population. This said, we used this reporter because it is very well established as an indirect measure of cortical activity, is amenable to chronic imaging over multiple sessions,  and, importantly, has a high signal to noise ratio (Romero et al 2019, Dana et al 2014). Due to the variability of cortical responses to sensory stimuli during most states of anesthesia (Stroh et al, 2022), we needed a high signal to noise ratio to maximize the number of useful trials and avoid the need to average over many successive trials to produce individual measurements. 

We extracted two quantitative measurements from aforementioned dF/F traces representing the activity of our region of interest within the cortex. These measurements were the peak intensity (maximum dF/F) and the response latency (time in seconds from stimulus to the peak intensity). The traces of the dF/F response typically rose to a single peak, though some traces showed multiple peaks. For consistency, we recorded the intensity of the first peak that surpassed a threshold of at least 70% of the maximum intensity in the trace(**Fig. 3C**). Trials were excluded if baseline levels of activity were higher than the activity measured after the stimulus. This would sometimes occur due to pre-stimulus activity from two sources. Firstly, the light used to excite the fluorescent reporter would often induce a visual response, resulting in a diffuse, wave-like pattern of fluorescence across the FOV of the cortex. This occurred despite efforts to cover the eyes of mice during imaging sessions and is likely due to the light illuminating the entire mouse skull through the optically transparent window, activating the retina with scattered light. This can be appreciated in sham trials in which no auditory stimulus was played (Fig\*). 

We delayed the onset of the auditory stimulus by 3 seconds to allow this visually evoked activity to diminish down to baseline levels. However, in a subset of trials, this visually evoked activity would last longer than 3 seconds and prevent detection of any auditory-stimulus induced activity. Secondly, it is likely that we induced a state of slow wave activity (SWA) with the use of isoflurane at levels of approximately 1.5-1.7% (Stroh et al 2022). This functional state would result in the slow, wavelike bursts of activity that appeared in some trials that would produce high levels of activity during the baseline window just prior to the stimulus. In these two cases, trials were omitted as we believe they do not reflect the response of the cortex to the auditory stimulus alone.   
After creating the traces of intensity as described above, we conducted one further processing step on the trace by transforming the data into a Z-score trace. This was achieved by taking the average of the baseline portion of the trace, subtracting that from every point on the trace, and then dividing the result by the standard deviation of the baseline portion of the trace. This was intended to correct for any traces in which the response was especially variable, which could sometimes result in an artificially high or low peak intensity. 

***Surgical Approach***  
Adult male mice between two to five months old were anesthetized with an induction dose of isoflurane at 5%, 2L/min followed by a maintenance dose of 2%, 2L/min. Induction doses were given in a closed chamber and maintenance dose was constantly given via a nose-cone affixed to the surgical plate. After transfering the mice from induction chamber to surgical plate, they were given one dose of Buprenorphrine (0.5mg/kg, delivered subcutaneously) to facilitate anesthesia maintenance and one dose of Dexamethasone (0.2mg/kg, delivered subcutaneously) to reduce inflammation. Temperature was maintained at 36 °C via a heating pad and temperature probe (40-90-8D, FHC, Inc., Bowdoin, ME). The eyes of each mouse were lubricated with petrolatum ophthalmic ointment (Puralube®, Fera Pharmaceuticals, Locust Valley, NY). All surgical procedures were performed under a surgical microscope (Zeiss S100 OPMI pico, Carl Zeiss Meditech AG, Jena, Germany). A 2mL bolus of lidocaine was injected under the skin overlying the scalp. Following a midline incision, the skin overlying the scalp was removed. The scalp was scored with a scalpel to promote adhesion of the head-mount. A custom built titanium bar was affixed to the left half of the exposed scalp with Loctite 454 and dental acrylic (C\&B Metabond, Parkell Inc., Edgewood, NY). After allowing the acrylic to set completely, the mouse was repositioned to allow removal of the skin overlying the right, temporal aspect of the skull. The skin was removed just caudal to the eye and rostral from the cartilaginous portion of the right ear. The muscles overlying the temporal aspect of the skull were detached from their insertion points on the superior aspect of the skull. A portion of the temporalis muscle was removed using two cuts. The first cut was done diagonally from a point near the caudal edge of the eye to a point near the eminence of the zygomatic arch. The second horizontal cut was made excising the remaining muscle inserting across the squamosal skull. A craniotomy measuring \~4x3mm was performed on the exposed skull overlying the auditory cortex (EXL-M40/LHP12 Drill, Osada Inc., Los Angeles, CA). The anatomical landmarks used to determine the excised region of skull were the extension of the zygomatic arch (ventral), the temporal ridge (dorsal), the lambdoid suture (caudal), and the squamosal suture (rostral). At least 1mm of skull was left exposed between the drilled edge of the craniotomy and the skin on all sides except ventrally, where remaining muscles below the squamosal aspect of the skull should not be disturbed due to the risk of inducing a serious bleed.  
![][image11]  
Fig.\* Schematic of surgical approach. Landmarks used: extension of zygomatic arch (ventral), temporal ridge (dorsal), lambdoid suture (caudal), and squamosal suture (rostral).

After sufficient thinning of craniotomy edges, forceps were used to delicately remove the isolated piece of skull without causing damage to the underlying tissue. Isotonic saline was applied to the exposed cortex to prevent damage from desiccation. Prior to surgery, cranial windows were prepared by cutting pieces from a large polymethylpentene sheet into rectangles slightly larger than 4x3mm. During surgery, the windows were kept in 70% ethanol. Just prior to removing the target skull region, the prepared windows were evaluated for fit to the drilled craniotomy by holding them just above the drilled portion of skull. The window was modified with sterile surgical scissors to fit if necessary. Windows were selected and/or cut to be slightly larger than the craniotomy and overlap with the remaining skull. The correctly-sized window was dried of any residual ethanol using compressed air. A non-toxic, optically clear silicone mixture (Kwik-Sil) was applied directly to the exposed cortex using a mixing tip applicator (World Precision Instruments, Sarasota, FL). Just prior to application, the drops of Kwik-Sil were examined for the presence of any air bubbles and discarded if any were present. A small drop of the Kwik-Sil (free of air bubbles) was placed in the center of the craniotomy. The dry window was immediately placed and lightly impressed on the drop of Kwik-Sil until the drop spread out to cover the exposed cortex and the overhanging edges of the window making contact with the skull. Loctite-454 was then applied in a ridge to just overlap the edges of the window, but not obscure view to any underlying cortex. The back of a sterile cotton tip applicator was used to flatten the Loctite ridge surrounding the window. This important step prevented any mounds of Loctite or acrylic from forming as that would excessively deform the fluid filled sack component of the ultrasound transducer that would eventually sit on top of the window during experiments. Drastic deformations in the plastic membrane could potentially disrupt the ultrasonic pulses traveling through the fluid-filled sack, especially when the deformations are in close proximity (edges of the window) to the targeted regions. Immediately after smoothing the surrounding Loctite ridge, an accelerant (Loctite Accelerator SF 712\) was applied in small drops to quickly solidify the covered region. After confirming that any exposed skull or Loctite was dry, C\&B Metabond dental acrylic was then applied generously to cover all exposed regions of skull, muscle, and the outer edge of the Loctite covered window. 

***Equipment and software***  
To capture focused images of the cortex through the implanted cranial windows in our mice, we used a wide-field, multi-zoom fluorescence macroscope (Nikon AZ100). The focal length of the microscope was accounted for in the design of our custom transducer which was mounted directly to the objective lens, such that both focuses coincided at the same plane. Illumination for fluorescence imaging was provided by an external halogen lamp (Lambda LS, Sutter Instruments, Novato, CA). This excitation light was filtered through a dichroic mirror to 470nm ± 20nm. During experiments, illumination was controlled with an external shutter, which was remotely commanded by triggers from a control panel in Labview (Uniblitz VMM-DI, Vincent Associates, Rochester, NY). Images were captured using a CMOS camera with accompanying software (Zyla, Andor Technology Ltd., Belfast, UK). For all calcium imaging experiments, frame rate was set to 18.57Hz to balance the temporal and optical resolution of the images without overloading our system. Timing of triggers was set in a custom LabVIEW workspace (National Instruments, Austin, TX). This workspace triggered the illumination shutter to open, initiation of capture (recording) from the CMOS camera, playing of pure tone auditory stimulus through the MF1 speaker (see next section), terminating the capture, and closing the illumination shutter. This sequence of triggers constituted an individual trial in which one video (100 frames per video) was captured.  
***Auditory stimuli***  
All auditory stimuli were played through the MF1 speaker in the closed-field configuration (Tucker Davis Technologies). A plastic tube of \~10cm with a tapered tip was led from the speaker and inserted into the mouse’s ear canal contralateral to the craniotomy. The speaker was powered and controlled with the RZ6 processing unit and accompanying software (SigCal, BioSig, RPvdsEx,Tucker Davis Technologies).  
 ![][image12]  
Fig.\* RZ6 processing unit and MF1 speaker. The speaker (bottom) was used in the closed configuration (right) for all acoustic stimuli in ABR and Calcium Imaging Experiments.

The speaker was calibrated before every experiment to account for changes in ambient temperature which could affect the speaker itself or the plastic connecting tube through which the acoustic waves could be amplified or attenuated. The speaker was calibrated to 1V \= 90.0dB by using SigCal software running at 200kHz sampling rate, with a calibrated range of frequencies from 5kHz to 33kHz. The calibration profile was converted into a frequency specific list of attenuation/gain adjustments so that the experimental software controlling the speaker output during imaging trials (RPvdsEx) could adjust each desired output tone to the desired dB level. This software was also used to attenuate the auditory stimulus from 90dB to 70dB for all trials across all experiments. 

**Chapter 5:  Effects of focused ultrasound on auditory-evoked neural responses**  
*short motivation for auditory studies; you will have reviewed some of this stuff in Introduction, but here you should note that none of the other neuromodulation studies have targeted aspects of cortical function that necessitated high resolution. Auditory is a good example of that and has a natural low-handing fruit target problem, i.e. tinnitus*.  
***Overview***

Prior studies investigating FUS neuromodulation on cortical activity have yet to demonstrate FUS induced changes in a context requiring high resolution. While FUS was shown to target highly localized subcortical targets in large animals, the smallest spatial extents of previously investigated targets measured 2mm (Dallapiaza et al 2017). Our experiments test an acoustic focus size of 0.5mm within a visible region of the mouse cortex measuring 2.6x2.6mm. In addition, the cortical target we use in our experiments is the location of a potential therapeutic application for FUS neuromodulation: Tinnitus. Tinnitus is defined as the perception of sounds, such as high pitched ringing, without the presence of auditory stimuli. As the number one disability reported in veterans and affecting over 25 million Americans each year, Tinnitus is a wide-spread complaint in need of treatment options (NIDCD, 2019). Tinnitus has a variety of causes, but many forms involve aberrant cortical activity within primary auditory cortex. Understanding and characterizing the FUS parameters capable of reliably modifying neural activity in auditory cortical neurons could directly contribute to therapies for human auditory conditions.  
While other cortices like the visual and motor cortex have been targeted with ultrasound to induce, excite, and suppress neural circuits *in vivo*, the auditory cortex remains an attractive but relatively less investigated testing bed for FUS neuromodulation. Auditory cortex has a distinct organization of sensory representation called “tonotopy” that is maintained at all levels of the auditory pathway (Merzenich et al 1973, Hacket et al 2011, Guo et al 2012, Peterson et al 2023\) . This tonotopic organization is present in humans as well as in many animal models, allowing for invasive *in vivo* experiments on neural circuits that have highly conserved connectivity and functional organization.  
The auditory system has been used to examine FUS neuromodulation in rats and pigs as described in the previous sections. The methods used to monitor activity in the auditory cortex in animals undergoing neuromodulation have been limited to subcutaneous electrode recordings of AEPs (Daniels et al 2019). The remaining literature on FUS’s effect on auditory cortical activity concerns a potential confound to FUS stimulation experiments in animal models. There have been reports of an auditory artifact during FUS stimulation of the visual cortex in small animals, where activity in the off-target auditory cortex was measured with calcium fluorescence imaging (Guo et al 2018, Sato et al 2018). These reports have been contested by experiments that showed FUS delivered to the cortex does not activate the peripheral auditory pathway if delivered with a smooth start/stop and that eliminating the peripheral auditory pathway with two different methods does not prevent FUS from stimulating target cortical regions (Mohammadjavadi et al 2019). This confound and the methods we used to address it are covered in the section on cranial window material selection in chapter 3\.

***Experimental groups***  
Mice were divided into three treatment groups and were presented with either 1\) the same 10kHz auditory stimulus for every trial during an experiment or 2\) alternating tones of 10kHz and 30kHz from the MF1 speaker. The three treatment groups were Control (No FUS), Low (0.625W/cm2 Isppa), and High (1.2W/cm2).   
![][image13]  
Fig.\*: Experimental timeline and treatment groups. Single tone experiments used 10kHz stimuli for all trials and have experiments in all three treatment groups. Dual tone experiments used 10kHz and 30kHz on alternating trials and have experiments in the control and High FUS group only. 

To analyze the results across the mice used in each experiment group, we calculated the changes from pre-treatment to post-treatment peak intensities and latencies for each mouse. This was done by taking the average peak intensity from the pre-treatment trials and subtracting that value from each post-treatment intensity measurement (likewise with the latency values). The resulting data represents the change in peak intensity or latency following the treatment, which was either no FUS (abbreviated “C” for control), low FUS (abbreviated “L”, 0.6W/cm2 Isppa), or high FUS (abbreviated “H”, 1.2W/cm2 Isppa). It should be noted that while the high FUS group is called “high” for simplicity in comparing the groups used in these experiments, the intensity used in this group is well below the limit for Low-intensity FUS which is commonly defined as FUS with spatial peak average intensity below 100W/cm2 Isppa. 

***Changes in peak fluorescence intensity following treatment***  
For statistical comparison, we first used a one-way ANOVA with Welch’s Correction to compare the results of each experiment *within an experiment group* (**Fig. 4**). For the peak intensity (with z-score correction), the results showed that the Controls (n=3 mice) were not significantly different from each other, the High FUS experiments (n=4 mice) were not significantly different from each other, and the Low FUS experiments (n=9 mice) had 2 experiments with means that differed significantly from the others (L7, L9).

For the Controls and High FUS experiments, we used this consistency across experiments to validate consolidating data points across all the experiments within a respective group (**Fig. 5**). For the Low FUS group, we present consolidated data with and without the data from the two experiments L7 and L9 (which differ from the other Low FUS experiments significantly). However, the significance does *not* change with their inclusion or exclusion in the aggregated comparison.   
The Low FUS group showed no difference in change in peak intensity from Controls with or without L7 and L9 contributing to the aggregate dataset (**Fig. 5, blue**). The High FUS group *did* show a highly significant difference in the change in peak intensity compared to controls, with the High FUS group having a higher mean change in peak fluorescence (**Fig. 5, red**).  The comparison between High FUS and Control was made within the ANOVA with a multiple comparisons procedure as well as in a separate T-test with Welch’s correction (graph not shown) which produced the same level of significance (see Fig.5 description). 

**Fig. 5** Mice treated with High FUS (1.2W/cm2 Isppa) showed increased peak fluorescence in response to acoustic stimuli after treatment, which was significantly different from controls. Left: Low FUS data includes data from experiments L7 and L9 whose means differ from the other Low FUS experiments. Right: L7 and L9 excluded. Results from ANOVA do not change. Grey: controls show reduction in peak fluorescence after the treatment phase (no FUS). Blue: mice treated with Low FUS (0.6W/cm2) do not show change in peak fluorescence after treatment that is significantly different from controls. Red: Mice treated with High FUS have significantly different changes in peak fluorescence after treatment compared to controls (p\<0.0001, Dunnett’s T3 Multiple Comparison; p\<0.0001, Unpaired T test with Welch’s correction ). 

***Changes in peak latency following treatment***  
In addition to the intensity value of the peak dF/F response trace, we measured the latency from the stimulus to the peak as well. We then calculated *changes* in peak latency in the same way as described in the previous section on peak intensity. While these intensity values showed no significant differences across experiments in the same treatment group (with exception of L7,L9; Fig\*), the latency values did dramatically change across experiments within the same treatment group. The variability in latency data from experiment to experiment resulted in significantly different distributions in the Control, Low-FUS, and High-FUS treatment groups: C1-C3 (Welch’s ANOVA Test, p\<0.0001), L1-L9 (Welch’s ANOVA Test, p\<0.0001), and H1-H4 (Welch’s ANOVA Test, p\<0.0001).The Low FUS group in particular shows this variability with nearly all multiple comparisons between individual experiments showing significantly different means.  
The variability of the latency data may reflect variations in cortical propagation velocity across individual mice. Incongruities in response latencies may also be exacerbated by low frame rate ( used during imaging sessions. Because of these differences within experiment groups, results from aggregate comparisons may not reflect differences in response latency solely due to the specific treatment applied in each group. This said, these comparisons are presented in **Fig. 7**. 

***Dual-stimuli experiments***  
In a subset of experiments, mice were stimulated with two auditory stimuli of different frequencies. The frequency of the tone would alternate on successive trials. Mice were presented with either tones of 10kHz or 30kHz (in the single-stimuli experiments, mice were always presented with a 10kHz tone).   
For these experiments there were two treatment groups: Control (no FUS, n=2) and High FUS (1.2W/cm2, n=4). The same processing and analysis method described in the prior section was applied to these experiments. However, only the 10kHz trials of control experiments were compared to 10kHz trials of the High FUS experiments. In the same way, 30kHz trials in the control experiments were compared to 30kHz trials in the HIgh FUS experiments. We found that High FUS treatment resulted in increased intensities measured in the 30kHz trials but not the 10kHz trials when compared to controls. 

**Fig. 8** Changes in intensity following treatment with High FUS (1.2W/cm2 Isppa). Left: For the 10kHz trials, there was no significant difference in response intensity after FUS treatment compared to controls (n \= Unpaired T Test, p \= 0.8932). Right: For the 30kHz trials, there was significant difference in response intensity (Unpaired T Test, p \= 0.0080). 

***Conclusions on observed FUS neuromodulation***  
We identified regions of the auditory cortex that respond strongly to pure tone stimuli. Using these regions as selective regions of interest, we quantified the responses to tones by the peak intensity of the calcium reporter fluorescence and the latency of the peak from the stimulus onset. We targeted these responsive regions with two levels of FUS and measured responses to tone stimuli after FUS treatment. The High FUS treatment group showed an increase in peak response intensity that was significantly different from controls. Intensity measurements were not significantly different across experiments within each treatment group, allowing aggregation of trials across mice in each group. Latency measurements, however, were highly variable from experiment to experiment, which should be considered in the comparison of controls to low and high FUS treatment groups (Fig. 7). In experiments where two different stimuli (10kHz or 30kHz tones) were used on alternating trials, the response to 10kHz tones did not change following High FUS treatment compared to 10kHz trials in control experiments. However, the trials where 30kHz tones were played did significantly increase in response intensity compared to the 30kHz trials in control experiments.   
These primary results reflect an important contribution to the growing understanding of FUS’s effect on neuronal and cortical activity. Efforts are being made to establish a consistent relationship between FUS parameters (such as Intensity; Isppa), and the resulting modulation of neural activity (Blackmore et al 2018, Zhang et al 2021, Pasquinelli et al 2019). While many studies show evidence of FUS inducing momentary excitation or suppression of neural activity (Tyler et al 2008, Yoo et al 2022), a subset of work has shown examples of FUS inducing a lasting effect on the neural targets (Fry et al 1958, Kim et al 2015, Daniels et al 2018).   
The results from our calcium imaging experiments provide an example of FUS induced enhancing modulation that affects cortical responses to sensory stimuli for 30-40 minutes following sonication. Our effective sonication parameters, specifically 50% Duty Cycle and 1.2W/cm2 Isppa, fall in line with previously used excitatory FUS parameters as well as theoretical models for the parameters driving FUS activation and suppression (Fisher et al 2018, Plaskin et al 2016, Blackmore et al, 2018, Zhang et al 2021). Secondly, our results provide evidence for FUS neuromodulation using a measurement that greatly improves in the spatial resolution from measurements used in other studies. Our approach allows us to appreciate cortical activity that spreads across a large swath of cortex spanning \~2.6mm and provides much more spatial information than somatosensory evoked responses measured with subcutaneous electrodes. In this way, we add to the body of knowledge on FUS’s modulatory effect at different parameter settings while improving on measurement techniques. 

**Chapter 6: Impact of sonication on tissue integrity and behavioral controls**  
***Overview***  
One of the potential adverse effects of FUS when applied to neural tissue, specifically in the CNS, are ultrasound induced disturbances of the microvasculature. Though the targets of FUS for neuromodulation are the neurons that lie within cortex, the myriad blood vessels supplying nutrients to those neurons (some with diameters as small as 10µm) also experience the ultrasonic pressure waves during sonication. While future studies will look into the effect of FUS on specific ion channels within endothelial and smooth muscle cells of cortical microvasculature, an immediate worry that we can evaluate is if our FUS parameters cause enough physical disturbance of the tissue so as to induce damage. This may present as ruptures of individual vessels or gross morphological alterations in the integrity of the target tissue. In addition, the CNS maintains a highly regulated blood brain barrier (BBB) which has been heavily studied in the context of FUS neuromodulation. FUS can be intentionally used to modulate the permeability of the BBB with the aid of additional techniques/reagents. For our purposes, however, we were primarily concerned with determining if the BBB had been disturbed at all at the levels of FUS used during neuromodulation experiments. To determine if the experimental levels of FUS we used in those experiments either caused gross damage to the target tissue and surrounding microvasculature or induced greater permeability to the BBB, we examined sonicated cortical targets *in vivo* with Optical coherence tomography-angiography (OCT-A) as well as ex vivo with immunohistochemistry imaging of sonicated brain slices following mouse injection with Evans blue dye. 

***Optical coherence tomography \- angiography***   
Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is an imaging technique in which infrared laser light is split into two identical beams that begin in phase with each other. One beam is directed through an imaging arm and onto the object being imaged. The other beam is directed through a reference arm and reflected back by a mirror. The imaging beam will penetrate the object being imaged, i.e. cortical tissue, and reflect off the various components and layers that make up the object.   
![][image14]  
Figure 1：Spectral Domain Optical Coherence Tomography (SD-OCT) Schematic setup. (A) Superluminescent light diode (central wavelength 840nm; bandwidth 160 nm). (B) 50:50 fiber coupler (Hi780, AC Photonics, Inc – CA). (C) Fiber collimator (F220APC-850, Thorlabs, Inc – NJ). (D) Galvanometer scanners (GVS002, Thorlabs, Inc – NJ). (E) Sample arm; Lens 1 \= 75 mm achromatic lens (AC508-075-B-ML, Thorlabs, Inc – NJ); Lens 2 \= 200 mm tube lens (TTL200-B, Thorlabs, Inc – NJ); 10x objective lens (M Plan Apo NIR 10x, Mitutoyo \- Japan). (F) Reference Arm; Dispersion compensation glass (LSM54DC1, Thorlabs, Inc – NJ); Lens 3 \= 50 mm achromatic lens (AC254-050-B-ML, Thorlabs, Inc – NJ). (G) Spectrometer (Cobra-S 800 OCT Spectrometers \- Wasatch Photonics, Logan, UT), Camera (Teledyne e2V \- OctoPlus).

The reflected light from these layers of tissue are affected by the different refractive properties of the layers (i.e. layers with vessels will reflect differently than layers with no vessels since vessel walls will absorb and reflect the incoming light differently than neural and glial tissue). As a result, the light returning from the object to the imaging arm will contain all of the reflected beams of each layer. This complex conflagration of light-waves will differ greatly in their phase, whereas the light in the reference arm will have the same phase as it started out with, having bounced off of a mirror. Comparing these two incoming sources of reflected light and partitioning the phase differences of the imaging arm, computer software can create image data of the object. The light from the imaging arm can be maneuvered using a system of mirrors on gimbals to traverse a square of tissue. As a result, OCT imaging can produce a scan of the imaged tissue that has width and height in the x and y plane, but also depth in the z plane as the light penetrates the layers of tissue. Lastly, OCT imaging can be additionally modified by scanning the same plane of tissue multiple times. Since the only moving component of the tissue should be the red blood cells (RBCs) traversing the cortical vasculature, the repeated scans can pick up on the motion blur of the RBCs.   
![][image15]![][image16]  
This effectively traces the path of the vessels, and can be used to highlight the microvasculature for a sharper image in a process called OCT-Angiography (OCT-A). With this method, we imaged the microvasculature of primary sensory cortex in the same mouse model used for calcium imaging experiments. We captured OCT-A images before sonication, 30 minutes after sonication, and 24 hours after sonication. The images were aligned by centering the OCT-A light beam’s center with the sonication target using physical landmarks on the cranial window as well as within the image. Thus, the center of the OCT-A images was the center of the FUS focus.  

![][image17]  
Fig.\*: OCT-A imaging at different time points with FUS treatment. All columns show the same three views of the layers imaged by the OCT-A laser. Red arrow: all layers superimposed. Blue arrow: a superficial layer close to the cortical surface. Yellow arrow: a layer near the depth limit of the imaged tissue voxel. A) Before sonication. B) 30 minutes after sonication. C) 24 hours after sonication. The sonication target in all images was the direct center of each image. 

OCT-A images across the depth of field revealed no gross morphological changes in vessel integrity at the targeted location (center of each image) or nearby regions. The findings from imaging were unremarkable 30 minutes following sonication as well as 24 hours after sonication. With these results, we posit that the FUS used during our experiments did not induce off-target changes in cortical vessels.   
***Immunohistochemistry and Evans Blue dye slice experiments***  
In order to assess if the BBB was disrupted with the previously used experimental levels of FUS for neuromodulation, we examined slices of fixed brains exposed to varying FUS intensities after the mice were infused with Evans blue dye. For these experiments, craniotomies \~3mm x 4mm were performed over the somatosensory cortex and covered with polymethylpentene window material as described in prior sections. After recovery and just prior to sonication, mice were injected with a bolus of Evans blue dye intraperitoneally (2% solution, 4mL/kg). Evans blue dye binds to serum albumin, which does not cross the BBB when the BBB is correctly maintained. If the brain were to be imaged in this state, Evans blue would be thus limited to the vasculature. If the BBB is perturbed and albumin is allowed to leak into the interstitial fluid of the brain, the Evans blue dye will show up in regions outside of vascular structures. After perfusion, I targeted two points with different levels of FUS within the window over the somatosensory cortex. The first point, approximately 0.5mm lateral to the midline suture, was sonicated with a very high intensity of FUS (\>200W/cm2 Isppa)  with the duty cycle set to 100% for 20 seconds. This high intensity and duty cycle were intended to cause damage at the FUS focus on this first point. This would serve as a positive control for gross damage to neural structures, microvasculature, and the BBB. A second point 0.5mm lateral to the first point was then sonicated at parameters previously used in calcium imaging experiments (50%DC, 1.2W/cm2 Isppa, 20 second duration). Immediately after sonication of the second point, the brain was extracted and fixed to be sliced coronally in sections \~18µm thick. Later, these sections were stained for GFAP, NG2, Evans blue, and CD31.   
![][image18]  
Fig.\* IHC slice fluorescent image showing the two targeted points after sonication. Red cross: targeted with FUS of \~400W/cm2 Isppa at 100% DC to induce damage. Structural damage is immediately appreciable without dyes, however evans blue (light red) fluorescence shows extensive leakage under the primary target and at the damaged invagination’s border. Blue cross: the secondary point targeted (\~500µm lateral to the primary point) with FUS of 1.2W/cm2 Isppa at 50% DC (higher level of neuromodulation parameters used in calcium imaging experiments). Tissue is in-tact and Evans blue is limited to the vasculature. Evans blue presence at the surface of the cortex is likely a result of staining from surface bleed during craniotomy.  

In addition to initial fluorescence imaging, confocal images were taken of the same slice, as well as slices rostral and caudal to the slice containing the center of the FUS target. Using these images, we determined that the extent of the affected region targeted with the high level of FUS was approximately 250-350µm.   
![][image19]  
Fig.\* Confocal images of slices containing damaged target. Slices caudal and rostral to the center of the damaged region show damage if they are within \~350µm. Regions beyond this show little to no signs of FUS induced damage. This reflects the known spatial characteristics of our transducer’s geometric focus from hydrophone recordings. The focus generated by our transducer has a full-width at half-maximum measuring 500µm.  

***Open field exploration tests***  
While off-target effects such as acute structural damage can be appreciated with slicing and image experiments, more complex alterations in cognition are another potential adverse effect of FUS application to cortical structures. To assess one potential cognitive side effect of sonication, we conducted open-field exploration tests on a subset of mice. Mice were allowed to explore a novel environment while their movements were monitored with an over-head camera. 

![][image20]  
Fig.\*: Schematic of open field testing apparatus. Sample image of video feed prior to movement tracking. 

The first group in this experiment consisted of mice that had undergone and recovered from the craniotomy procedure, but had not gone through any FUS sonication (control group). The second group consisted of mice that had undergone sonication at 22W/cm2 Isppa at 50% DC. This was a fairly high level of sonication intensity, though the duty cycle remained at 50%. While high in comparison to our experimental values of 1.2W/cm2 Isppa for neuromodulation experiments, 22W/cm2 is still within FUS parameters tested by other groups (Plaskin et al, 2016). Videos were initially taken using a DSLR camera (PENTAX K-70, Ricoh Imaging Company LTD). Later experiments used the camera of an Iphone 13 which was more easily positioned directly over the exploration chamber. A single, white paper sheet was cut to size and placed at the bottom of the chamber to provide significant contrast between the mouse and chamber floor. We used a Matlab based script to output traces of mouse movement and cumulative distance traveled.
